source_paper_title: Variational Inequalities on Unbounded Domains for Zero-Sum Singular Controller vs. Stopper Games
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Bovo_2025, title={Variational Inequalities on Unbounded Domains for Zero-Sum Singular Controller vs. Stopper Games}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0029}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0029}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Bovo, Andrea and De Angelis, Tiziano and Issoglio, Elena}, year={2025}, month=feb, pages={277–312} }
context:
Page 7, Section 3 (Remark 3.5).

Uniqueness of the solution to Problem A remains an open question. Methods used in, e.g., [35], do not apply due to the presence of obstacle and gradient constraints.
title: Uniqueness of Sobolev solutions to min-max variational inequality with obstacle and gradient constraints
background_latex:
Fix a finite horizon \(T\in(0,\infty)\) and dimension \(d\in\mathbb{N}\). Let \(b:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^d\) and \(\sigma:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^{d\times d'}\) be continuously differentiable, locally Lipschitz, with at most linear growth, and let \(a(x):=\sigma(x)\sigma(x)^\top\). Assume local ellipticity: for every bounded \(B\subset\mathbb{R}^d\) there exists \(\theta_B>0\) such that \(\langle \zeta, a(x)\zeta\rangle\ge \theta_B\|\zeta\|_d^2\) for all \(x\in B\), \(\zeta\in\mathbb{R}^d\).

Define the second-order operator acting on sufficiently regular \(\varphi\) by
\[
(L\varphi)(x)=\tfrac12\mathrm{tr}\big(a(x)D^2\varphi(x)\big)+\langle b(x),\nabla \varphi(x)\rangle.
\]
Let \(r\ge 0\) be a constant. Let \(f,g,h:[0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d\to[0,\infty)\) be continuous, with the regularity and growth conditions ensuring the expressions below are well-defined almost everywhere for Sobolev functions: specifically assume \(g\) and \(f^2\) are locally \(C^{1,2,\alpha}\) and \(h\) is locally \(C^{0,1,\alpha}\) for some \(\alpha\in(0,1)\), and \(g(t,x)+h(t,x)\) has at most quadratic growth in \(x\). Assume also the compatibility condition
\[
\|\nabla g(t,x)\|_d\le f(t,x),\qquad (t,x)\in[0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d.
\]

For \(p>d+2\), consider functions \(u\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d)\). By Sobolev embedding, such \(u\) have a locally H\"older-continuous spatial gradient, so the pointwise constraints \(u\ge g\) and \(\|\nabla u\|_d\le f\) are meaningful.

Define the (parabolic) min-max variational inequality on \([0,T)\times\mathbb{R}^d\) with terminal condition \(u(T,x)=g(T,x)\):
\[
\min\Big\{\,\max\big\{\partial_t u + Lu - r u + h,\; g-u\big\},\; f-\|\nabla u\|_d\,\Big\}=0\quad \text{a.e. on }[0,T)\times\mathbb{R}^d.
\]
A solution is understood in the strong (Sobolev) sense, i.e. the PDE terms hold almost everywhere, with \(u\) satisfying the obstacle and gradient constraints and the terminal condition.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether the above min-max variational inequality with terminal condition \(u(T,x)=g(T,x)\) admits at most one solution \(u\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d)\) (for some/every \(p>d+2\)) satisfying the quadratic-growth bound \(|u(t,x)|\le c(1+\|x\|_d^2)\) for a constant \(c>0\).

Equivalently, prove or disprove: if \(u_1,u_2\in W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d)\) both satisfy
1) \(u_i(T,x)=g(T,x)\) for all \(x\in\mathbb{R}^d\),
2) \(u_i\ge g\) and \(\|\nabla u_i\|_d\le f\) pointwise on \([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d\),
3) \(\min\{\max\{\partial_t u_i + Lu_i - r u_i + h,\; g-u_i\},\; f-\|\nabla u_i\|_d\}=0\) a.e. on \([0,T)\times\mathbb{R}^d\),
4) \(|u_i(t,x)|\le c(1+\|x\|_d^2)\),
then \(u_1\equiv u_2\) on \([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d\).

1) \(u_i(T,x)=g(T,x)\) for all \(x\in\mathbb{R}^d\),
2) \(u_i\ge g\) and \(\|\nabla u_i\|_d\le f\) pointwise on \([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d\),
3) \(\min\{\max\{\partial_t u_i + Lu_i - r u_i + h,\; g-u_i\},\; f-\|\nabla u_i\|_d\}=0\) a.e. on \([0,T)\times\mathbb{R}^d\),

then \(u_1\equiv u_2\) on \([0,T]\times\mathbb{R}^d\).
open_problem_quote: Uniqueness of the solution to Problem A remains an open question. Methods used in, e.g., [35], do not apply due to the presence of obstacle and gradient constraints.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Non-uniqueness (or uniqueness) is a universal analytical property of a PDE/variational inequality on an unbounded continuous domain; there is no obvious finite, discretizable instance family whose verification would constitute a rigorous counterexample without additional structure.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could explore discretizations or restricted parametric families of coefficients/payoffs to search numerically for multiple solutions or to test candidate comparison principles, but translating numerical evidence into a rigorous uniqueness proof/counterexample would still require substantial human-led analysis.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Bovo–De Angelis–Issoglio) establishes existence of a strong Sobolev solution with quadratic growth to the parabolic min-max variational inequality \(\min\{\max\{\partial_t u+Lu-ru+h,\ g-u\},\ f-\|\nabla u\|\}=0\) on \([0,T)\times\mathbb R^d\) under local ellipticity and compatibility \(\|\nabla g\|\le f\). The solution is obtained as a limit of doubly-penalised semilinear PDEs on expanding bounded cylinders, with delicate uniform gradient bounds and bounds on the penalty terms. The resulting limit is shown to be the maximal Sobolev solution and coincides with the value of the associated singular-controller vs stopper game.

However, uniqueness in the class \(W^{1,2,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}\) with quadratic growth remains open (Remark 3.5). Classical comparison/uniqueness arguments for gradient-constrained singular control (e.g., Soner–Shreve’s parabolic free-boundary analysis) do not directly apply because the equation combines both an obstacle constraint and a gradient constraint in a min-max structure, and is posed on an unbounded domain with only local ellipticity. Related analytic works (Evans; Hynd; Kelbert–Moreno-Franco) provide techniques for gradient constraints and penalisation, but do not resolve comparison for this mixed obstacle/gradient min-max variational inequality. Promising directions include developing a comparison principle tailored to the nested \(\min\)-\(\max\) operator in a strong Sobolev setting (possibly via monotonicity methods or a game-theoretic dynamic programming/comparison argument) and identifying additional structural conditions (e.g., convexity in \(x\), uniform ellipticity, or stronger coercivity/growth controls) under which uniqueness can be proved.

sources.title: Variational Inequalities on Unbounded Domains for Zero-Sum Singular-Controller vs. Stopper Games
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves existence of a maximal strong Sobolev solution with quadratic growth to the parabolic min-max variational inequality with obstacle constraint \(u\ge g\) and gradient constraint \(\|\nabla u\|\le f\) on \([0,T]\times\mathbb R^d\), via double penalisation and compactness; establishes game value representation and maximality but leaves uniqueness open.
sources.connection: This is the source paper formulating the open uniqueness question (Remark 3.5) for strong Sobolev solutions of the min-max variational inequality on an unbounded domain under local ellipticity and quadratic growth.

sources.title: A second order elliptic equation with gradient constraint
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces penalisation and a priori estimates for second-order PDEs with gradient constraints, establishing existence/regularity frameworks that became standard in singular control HJB equations.
sources.connection: Provides foundational analytic techniques (penalisation, gradient bounds) used in the source paper; however it does not address the parabolic min-max structure with simultaneous obstacle and gradient constraints nor uniqueness on \(\mathbb R^d\).

sources.title: A free boundary problem related to singular stochastic control: the parabolic case
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies parabolic variational inequalities/free-boundary problems arising from singular stochastic control with gradient constraints; develops comparison/uniqueness and regularity in settings without an additional obstacle constraint.
sources.connection: Often-cited uniqueness/comparison methods for gradient-constrained problems, but the source paper notes these methods do not directly extend to the combined obstacle+gradient min-max inequality on unbounded domains.

sources.title: HJB equations with gradient constraint associated with controlled jump-diffusion processes
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes existence/regularity for HJB equations with gradient constraints for controlled jump-diffusions using penalisation and Sobolev techniques.
sources.connection: Technically close on the gradient-constraint side and uses similar penalisation ideas, but does not incorporate the additional obstacle/min-max structure or address uniqueness for strong Sobolev solutions under quadratic growth on \(\mathbb R^d\).

sources.title: Analysis of Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equations arising in stochastic singular control
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides PDE analysis for HJB equations from singular control, including gradient constraints, focusing on existence/regularity and structural properties.
sources.connection: Relevant for analytic tools around gradient constraints; does not settle uniqueness for the parabolic min-max obstacle+gradient variational inequality in Sobolev class on unbounded domains.

sources.title: A zero-sum game between a singular stochastic controller and a discretionary stopper
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes one-dimensional singular controller vs stopper zero-sum games; provides verification and explicit solutions in examples, highlighting potential nonsmoothness of the value.
sources.connection: Motivates the min-max obstacle/gradient structure from controller-stopper games; however results are largely 1D and do not provide uniqueness for multidimensional Sobolev solutions of the associated variational inequality.
